note: JMS lost Minneapolis service ~2013 when Great Lakes ended service; only Denver (United Express/SkyWest) remains under EAS

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Dickinson Theodore Roosevelt Regional Airport — Delta Connection flew to Minneapolis-St. Paul until November 30, 2015
Wikipedia: Dickinson Theodore Roosevelt Regional Airport history — first airline flights were Frontier DC-3s 1959
Wikipedia: Dickinson Theodore Roosevelt Regional Airport; Big Sky replaced Frontier's Twin Otter service linking Billings to far western ND towns

note: DIK lost Minneapolis service Nov 30, 2015; original Frontier Airlines ceased 1986; currently serves only Denver under EAS

Wikipedia: Brookings Regional Airport — Great Lakes Aviation EAS service to Minneapolis replaced by Mesa Airlines in fall 2004

note: BKX lost all scheduled service September 2009 when DOT ended Essential Air Service subsidy; now general aviation only

lostDestinations.source: Business View Magazine: Huron Regional Airport — lost service 2016; Great Lakes exceeded per-passenger subsidy cap when using 9-seat aircraft
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport — in 1981 Northern Airlines flew Huron to Minneapolis, Sioux Falls, Rapid City and Pierre
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport history — North Central/Republic/Northwest served multi-stop routes through Sioux Falls
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport — Northern Airlines 1981 flew to Pierre; Republic left 1981-82
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport — Air Midwest served Huron

note: HON lost all commercial service 2016; formerly served by Western, North Central, Republic, Northern, Mesaba, GP Express, Air Midwest, and Great Lakes Airlines

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — Great Lakes Airlines flew Minneapolis and Denver until ceasing all service late 2015
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — during 1970s Western Boeing 737-200s flew nonstop to Rapid City and Sioux Falls; Western left 1984
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — Western flew nonstop Pierre-Sioux Falls until 1984
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — 1970s Western 737-200s flew with direct service to Salt Lake City; Western left 1984
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — fall 1994 AirVantage Metroliners flew nonstop to Bismarck, Rapid City, and Sioux Falls

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Watertown Regional Airport — Republic Airlines left Watertown in 1985
Wikipedia: Watertown Regional Airport — North Central DC-9s flew Minneapolis-Watertown-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City route; Republic left 1985
Wikipedia: Watertown Regional Airport — 2007 Mesaba Saab 340s flew direct to Minneapolis via Watertown; Great Lakes had prior Pierre connections
Wikipedia: Watertown Regional Airport — North Central/Republic operated Minneapolis-Watertown-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City route until 1985

note: ATY lost Chicago (ORD) and Denver service in 2022-2025 transitions; Denver Air Connection served 2021-2025; SkyWest returned August 2025 to Denver

lostDestinations.source: Simple Flying / airportimprovement.com — airport saw 11 flights/day including Houston nonstop at height of oil boom 2014
Wikipedia: Sloulin Field International Airport — oil boom peak service; enplanements peaked at 119,069 in 2014 then declined

detail: Sloulin Field (ISN) closed October 10, 2019 and was replaced by new Williston Basin International (XWA); at oil-boom peak in 2014 the airport served 119,069 passengers with 11 daily flights including Houston nonstop, then service contracted sharply after oil prices fell.
source: Wikipedia: Williston Basin International Airport; airportimprovement.com oil boom article

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Minot International Airport — Frontier discontinued service from Minot in 2015 due to airline restructuring
Wikipedia: Minot International Airport — original Frontier served route connecting Saskatoon, Regina, and Denver; ceased with bankruptcy 1986
Wikipedia: Minot International Airport history

detail: Minot retains Delta Connection to Minneapolis and United Express to Denver, but lost Frontier Airlines Denver service in 2015 (restructuring) and the original Frontier's Canada routes when that airline went bankrupt in 1986.

lostDestinations.source: Grand Forks International Airport website / Wikipedia — United Express started Denver service October 3, 2012; terminated December 3, 2013 citing low load factors
Wikipedia: Grand Forks International Airport — Great Lakes operated United Express hubs at Chicago O'Hare and Denver in 1992-2002 period
GFK airport website — currently served by Delta Connection

detail: Grand Forks lost United Express Denver service December 3, 2013 after only 14 months of service due to low load factors; currently retains Delta Connection to Minneapolis and Allegiant leisure routes but lost hub connections to Chicago and Denver.
source: Wikipedia: Grand Forks International Airport; gfkairport.com

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Mitchell Municipal Airport — Northwest Airlink pulled out in 1991; facility was passenger terminal until 1991
Wikipedia: Mitchell Municipal Airport — North Central DC-3s flew Omaha routing including Mitchell; Republic left ~1982
Wikipedia: Mitchell Municipal Airport — Mesaba flew Huron-Mitchell-Sioux Falls routing in 1982

detail: Mitchell Municipal Airport lost all scheduled commercial service in 1991 when Northwest Airlink (Mesaba) withdrew; airline service began ~1951 with Midwest Airlines and Braniff, then North Central arrived 1959, with Republic leaving ~1982 and Northwest Airlink departing 1991.

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Chan Gurney Municipal Airport — in April 2001 United Express dropped its flight to Denver
Wikipedia: Chan Gurney Municipal Airport — Yankton dropped out of OAG 1989-1990; Republic left ~1982
Wikipedia: Chan Gurney Municipal Airport — AAA Airlines flew nonstop to Sioux Falls and Norfolk NE

detail: Chan Gurney Municipal Airport lost its final scheduled service in April 2001 when United Express dropped the Denver flight; North Central began service in 1957-58, Republic left ~1982, and the airport dropped from OAG ~1989-90 before United Express briefly restored Denver service.

lostDestinations.source: Business View Magazine: Huron Regional Airport — Great Lakes flew EAS to Minneapolis; lost EAS subsidy when exceeded $1,000/passenger cap with 9-seat aircraft
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport — Northern Airlines 1981 flew Huron to Minneapolis, Sioux Falls, Rapid City, Pierre; Republic left end of 1981
Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport — Air Midwest listed among carriers
Wikipedia: Brookings Regional Airport — Mesa Airlines flew Huron-Brookings-Omaha EAS route until 2009 EAS subsidy ended

detail: Huron Regional Airport lost all scheduled commercial service in 2016 when Great Lakes Airlines' EAS contract was terminated after the per-passenger subsidy exceeded the $1,000 cap; the airport had peak service from Western and North Central airlines in the 1960s-70s serving up to 30,000 passengers per year.
source: Wikipedia: Huron Regional Airport; Business View Magazine Huron Regional Airport article

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — Great Lakes flew Minneapolis and Denver until ceasing all service late 2015
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — Great Lakes Airlines (United Express) Beech 1900s flew nonstop to Denver; ceased late 2015
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — Western Boeing 737-200s flew with direct service to Salt Lake City until 1984
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — 1970s Western 737s flew nonstop Pierre-Rapid City-Sioux Falls with direct service to Denver-Salt Lake City-Los Angeles; Western left 1984
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport — fall 1994 AirVantage Metroliners flew Pierre-Rapid City-Bismarck; Mesaba served 1984-1994

detail: Pierre Regional Airport lost all scheduled commercial service in late 2015 when Great Lakes Airlines ceased operations; the airport had DC-9 mainline jet service from North Central/Republic and Western Airlines through 1984, with commuter service continuing until Great Lakes' final departure.
source: Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport; city of Pierre official website

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport (cross-reference) — Great Lakes United Express operated hubs at Chicago O'Hare and Denver; Aberdeen was on North Central DC-9 Minneapolis-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City route
Wikipedia: Great Lakes Airlines — operated United Express at Chicago O'Hare hub 1992-2002; Aberdeen was within route network
Wikipedia: Watertown Regional Airport — North Central/Republic DC-9s flew Minneapolis-Watertown-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City; Republic left 1985

detail: Aberdeen retains Delta Connection to Minneapolis (sole current service) but lost Denver and Chicago connections when Great Lakes Airlines ended United Express operations in 2002; Republic Airlines served Aberdeen on the classic Minneapolis-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City DC-9 route until 1985.
source: Wikipedia: Aberdeen Regional Airport; Aberdeen Regional Airport history page

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Brookings Regional Airport — Mesa Airlines flew EAS route Huron-Brookings-Omaha; DOT ended subsidy September 2009
Wikipedia: Brookings Regional Airport — Great Lakes Aviation EAS service to Minneapolis replaced by Mesa Airlines in fall 2004

detail: Brookings Regional Airport lost all scheduled air service in September 2009 when the US DOT ended its Essential Air Service subsidy; the airport was previously served via EAS with routes to Minneapolis (Great Lakes through 2004) and Omaha (Mesa Airlines 2004-2009).
source: Wikipedia: Brookings Regional Airport; DOT EAS program records

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia: Black Hills Airport — Western Airlines served Spearfish 1949-50 until 1959
Wikipedia: Pierre Regional Airport cross-reference — North Central DC-3s flew Minneapolis-Watertown-Aberdeen-Pierre-Rapid City-Spearfish SD route 1959

detail: Black Hills Airport (Spearfish) lost all scheduled commercial service around 1959 when Western Airlines withdrew; the airport was dedicated in 1934 and now serves as the largest general aviation airport west of the Missouri River in South Dakota.
